message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 15
Recent changes: Green phase added NO production code — "cursed sword + plain amulet → 231" confirmed the per-item surcharge scope was already correct.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules, APP mass before/after. No code changed; confirm cleanliness or make a small justified improvement only. Do not implement ahead (loyalty/follow-up contract modifiers and claims not yet tested).

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 15 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the code.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing every name in `claim-office.ts`:

- `Item`, `QuoteStep`, `Scenario` — domain types, clearly named.
- `BASE_PREMIUM` — record of base premiums by type. Clear.
- `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`, `PROCESSING_FEE` — match the spec/test vocabulary ("first-insurance", "processing fee"). Clear.
- `COMPONENT_TYPES`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE`, `COMPONENT_BLOCK_PREMIUM` — describe the rune/moonstone block-discount rule. Clear.
- `isComponent(type)` — predicate, well-named.
- `typeBasePremium(type, count)` — base premium for `count` items of a given type, applying the block rule. Accurate.
- `CURSE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE` — match modifier vocabulary. Clear.
- `surchargeForItem(item)` — per-item surcharge (curse + high-enchantment). Reveals intent precisely, and the just-confirmed test ("cursed sword + plain amulet → 231") validates that surcharges are computed per item, which this name correctly conveys.
- `itemSurcharges(items)` — sum of per-item surcharges. Clear.
- `countByType(items)` — counts items by type. Clear.
- `policyBasePremium(items)` — total base premium across the policy. Clear distinction from `typeBasePremium`.
- `quotePremium(step)` — final premium for a quote step. Clear.
- `runScenario(scenario)` — entry point. Clear.

Naming assessment: every name reveals intent and matches the test/spec vocabulary. No rename needed. The recent Green cycle added no code and confirmed the per-item scoping, which `surchargeForItem` already names correctly.

## Step 2: Initial APP Mass

Computing mass across the functions (constants×1, bindings×1, invocations×2, conditionals×4, loops×5, assignments×6):

- `isComponent`: 1 binding + 1 invocation = 1 + 2 = **3**
- `typeBasePremium`: bindings (type, count) = 2; invocations (isComponent, includes-implicit, BASE_PREMIUM lookup, `count*`...) ~ isComponent call (2), `count === SIZE` conditional (4), `count * BASE_PREMIUM[type]` (1 binding access + 1 mult invocation ≈ 2); constants referenced as bindings. Roughly 2 + 2 + 4 + 2 ≈ **10**
- `surchargeForItem`: binding `base` (1), `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` access (2), curse ternary (4) + mult (2), highEnchantment `??` (treated ~conditional 4) + `>=` conditional (4) + mult (2), final `+` (2) ≈ **~21**
- `itemSurcharges`: reduce loop (5) + invocation surchargeForItem (2) + bindings ≈ **~9**
- `countByType`: explicit `for` loop (5) + `set`/`get` invocations (2+2) + assignment via set + `??` (4) ≈ **~14**
- `policyBasePremium`: reduce loop (5) + typeBasePremium invocation (2) + spread (2) ≈ **~10**
- `quotePremium`: bindings + invocations (policyBasePremium 2, itemSurcharges 2, Math.ceil 2) + arithmetic ≈ **~12**
- `runScenario`: map loop (5) + invocation (2) ≈ **~8**

Total ≈ **~87**. The values are dominated by inherent loops (one per aggregation) and conditionals (one per business rule), not by accidental complexity.

## Step 3: Four Rules of Simple Design

**Rule 1 — Tests pass**: All 15 green. Confirmed.

**Rule 2 — Reveals intent**: Strong. Named constants for every magic number, predicate extracted, functions read as the domain rules. No improvement found.

**Rule 3 — No duplication**: Checked for repeated structure. `typeBasePremium` and `surchargeForItem` both look up `BASE_PREMIUM[type]`, but these are distinct concepts (base price vs. modifier base) — consolidating would couple unrelated rules and harm clarity (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3). The two aggregation helpers (`itemSurcharges`, `policyBasePremium`) use reduce differently and aren't duplication. No actionable duplication.

**Rule 4 — Fewest elements**: Every function is referenced and each carries one clear responsibility. `countByType` is used only by `policyBasePremium`, but inlining it would push an imperative loop into an otherwise declarative function and reduce clarity. No element is gratuitous.

## Step 4: Refactoring Attempt

I evaluated one candidate small improvement: in `surchargeForItem`, the `?? 0` default plus the `>= threshold` comparison. Restructuring (e.g., extracting an `isHighEnchantment` predicate) would add an element (Rule 4 cost) for marginal clarity gain, and would raise rather than lower APP mass. Mass-reducing rewrites (collapsing the ternaries into arithmetic) would obscure the business rules — a Rule 2 regression, which trumps APP. None of the candidate changes improves a metric without regressing a higher-priority rule.

Per the mandate to attempt at least one refactoring, I made the attempt explicit and rejected each candidate on principled grounds rather than applying a change that would degrade clarity or element count.

## Step 5 & 6: Decision

No code changed. The implementation is already clean: naming reveals intent and matches the freshly-confirmed per-item surcharge scope, there is no actionable duplication, and the element count is minimal. The remaining APP mass is intrinsic to the domain rules (one loop per aggregation, one conditional per business rule), not accidental complexity. Every reduction I could construct would trade clarity (Rule 2) for lower mass (APP), which the priority order forbids. I intentionally did **not** implement the untested loyalty/follow-up/claims rules.

## Step 7: Completion Report

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: None applied. Candidate changes (extract `isHighEnchantment`, collapse surcharge ternaries) were evaluated and rejected — each would either add an element (Rule 4) or sacrifice clarity (Rule 2) for marginal/negative APP gain.
- **Naming**: Evaluated first; all names reveal intent and align with the per-item surcharge scope the latest test confirmed. No rename warranted.
- **APP Mass**: ~87, unchanged (before == after). Mass is intrinsic to the domain (one loop per aggregation, one conditional per rule).
- **Tests**: All 15 passing (21 todo remain). Green.

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_06-28-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
